doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: No nodules requiring follow-up are identified.

Discoid atelectasis in both lungs. Some bronchial walls are irregularly thickened, with bronchial contents in the lumens (predominantly at the level of both lower lobes). No pleural effusion. No areas of abnormal contrast enhancement are identified.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No masses, areas of abnormal contrast enhancement, or lymphadenopathy are identified.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification is present in the left circumflex and right coronary arteries, qualitatively mild.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with mild wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated. No pulmonary arterial contrast filling defects are identified.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Demineralization of bone tissue.

Upper abdomen: Splenic artery aneurysm measuring up to 1.2 × 0.9 cm is identified. No areas of abnormal contrast enhancement are identified.

Thyroid gland: Nodules measuring up to 5 mm and density up to 127 HU are present. Margins are fairly well defined.
doc_impression:
1. No nodules requiring follow-up per Fleischner Society recommendations. Bronchial contents and bronchial wall thickening in both lungs, which may correspond to bronchitis.

2. Coronary calcification, qualitatively mild. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

3. Mild atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta. No pulmonary arterial contrast filling defects are identified.

4. Splenic artery aneurysm.

5. Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Demineralization of bone tissue.

6. Thyroid nodules; per "Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the ACR Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee, 2014", no further follow-up is required.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: A few solid micronodules measure 2 mm in both lungs (series 5, images 78, 153, 164). Discoid atelectasis in the right middle and lower lobes. Bronchial walls are irregularly thickened, with bronchial contents in the lumens (predominantly in both lower lobes), air trapping due to obstructed airways. No pleural effusion. No areas of abnormal contrast enhancement are identified.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No masses, areas of abnormal contrast enhancement, or lymphadenopathy are identified.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. It is impossible to assess the presence of coronary calcium without a non-contrast series.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated. No pulmonary arterial contrast filling defects are identified.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Severe demineralization of bone tissue is present (decreased density of vertebral bodies: T12 - 39 HU, L1 - 38 HU, L2 - 38 HU).

Upper abdomen: Splenic artery aneurysm measuring up to 1.2 cm × 9 mm is identified, unchanged. Signs of nutcracker syndrome are identified with the pre-compression left renal vein measuring 8 mm in diameter and 3 mm at the level of compression (compression ratio 2.6, previously 2.0), along with a reduced aortic-SMA angle of approximately 20 degrees. In this setting, there is dilatation of the left gonadal vein up to 9 mm (previously 8 mm).

Thyroid gland: Multiple nodules measure up to 5 mm in both lobes. ACR White Paper recommendations (Hoang, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2015;12:143-150) suggest the following: No need for further follow-up.
val_impression:
1. A few solid micronodules in both lungs. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of lung cancer, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional follow-up chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion. If unchanged, no further follow-up is necessary.

2. Bronchial contents and bronchial wall thickening in both lungs, which may correspond to bronchitis. Air trapping due to obstructed airways.

3. No pulmonary arterial contrast filling defects are identified. It is impossible to assess the presence of coronary calcium without a non-contrast series.

4. Severe demineralization of bone tissue (osteoporosis).

5. Splenic artery aneurysm, unchanged. ACR White Paper recommendations (Khosa, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2013;10:789-794) suggest the following: Follow-up evaluation with CT or MRI annually.

6. Signs of nutcracker syndrome with left gonadal vein dilatation up to 9 mm. According to the ACR White Paper recommendations (Khosa, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2013;10:789-794) in the absence of hypertension, hematuria, proteinuria, or symptoms of pelvic congestion syndrome, follow-up is generally not required.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_protocol:
INDICATION: Coughing 11 days.

COMPARISON: Abdomen CT on May 9, 2023.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax with IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 6.33 mGy; total DLP 239.68 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura:
Linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe, lingular segments, and in lung bases bilaterally.
Areas of pulmonary emphysema (more on the right). 11.
In the left upper lobe (series 5), a ground-glass nodule up to 1.1 × 1.1 cm (image 125) persists, with no interval change on independent repeat measurement.
A few perifissural solid nodule on the right up to 3 mm.
No pleural effusion.
The trachea and main bronchi are patent.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:
No mass or lymphadenopathy is identified.

Heart:
No cardiomegaly.
No clinically significant pericardial effusion.
Aortic valve calcification.
Mild coronary calcification (left coronary artery, left anterior descending artery). The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

Great vessels:
The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with wall calcification.
The main pulmonary artery is dilated up to 35 mm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:
Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation.
Decreased bone density in the vertebrae at the T11 level to +148 HU.

Upper abdomen:
In the splenic parenchyma (inferior portions), a hypodense lesion with ill-defined margins measures up to 1.4 × 1.2 cm with density of approximately +35 HU.
Areas of fatty replacement of the pancreas.
doc_impression:
1. Solitary ground-glass nodule in the left upper lobe (per Fleischner Society 2017 recommendations, CT follow-up per scheme: CT in 6–12 months to confirm persistence of the nodule, then CT every 2 years until 5 years). Pulmonary emphysema. Perifissural solid nodule on the right.

2. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.5 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

3. Mild coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Aortic valve calcification.

5. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

6. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Low bone density at the T11 level.

6. Hypodense splenic lesion with ill-defined margins (correlate with MRI findings). Pancreatic lipomatosis.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Multiple linear atelectasis in lung bases bilaterally, a few atelectasis in the right middle lobe, left lingula. A few small cysts in the lower lobe of the right lung, unchanged, clinically insignificant. In the left upper lobe a ground-glass nodule up to 10 × 7 mm (series 6, image 251), previously on November 6, 2025, 10 × 6 mm, stable compared to October 28, 2024. A few stable perifissural nodules (lymph nodes) on the right up to 3 mm, require no follow-up. No pleural effusion. The trachea and main bronchi are patent.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy is identified.

Heart: The heart is slightly enlarged due to increased size of the chambers. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Aortic valve calcification. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is dilated up to 35 mm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Bone islands of T12, T6 vertebral bodies, unchanged.

Upper abdomen: In the splenic parenchyma (inferior portions), a hypodense lesion with ill-defined margins measures up to 1.4 × 1.2 cm with density of approximately +21 HU, unchanged compared to October 28, 2024. For stable splenic mass with nondiagnostic features, ACR White Paper recommendations (Heller, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2013;10:833-839) suggest the following: No need for further follow-up.
val_impression:
1. Solitary ground-glass nodule in the left upper lobe, stable compared to November 6, 2025 and October 28, 2024. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: Chest CT every 2 years, until 5 years after the initial discovery of the nodule.

2. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.5 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

3. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs.

4. Aortic valve calcification. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

COMPARISON: Chest CT on November 6, 2025, October 28, 2024.

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 11 mGy; total DLP 478.92 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 1.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Pleuroapical scarring, greater on the right. Centrilobular emphysema in the upper lobes.  
Linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe, lingula, and in lung bases bilaterally.

A few solid pulmonary nodules are present in series 5 as follows:

In the right lung:
- In the middle lobe:
  - Subpleural nodule measuring up to 5 mm (image 150).
- In the lower lobe:
  - Nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 137).
In the left lung:
- In the upper lobe:
  - Nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 90), calcified.
In the right upper lobe, areas of fibrotic bands at the level of images 36-85 (series 5) with a few calcifications up to 7 mm.  
No pleural effusion.  
The trachea and main bronchi are patent.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No masses or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly.  
No significant pericardial effusion.  
Aortic valve calcification.  
Mild coronary calcification (left anterior descending artery). The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with wall calcification.  
The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation.  
Transpedicular fixation at T1-T2. Decreased density of bone tissue in the vertebrae at T11-L1 to 110 HU.

Upper abdomen: Unremarkable.  
Included soft tissues at the level of the examination: In the breasts (partially included in the scan range), multiple partially calcified lesions measuring up to 9 mm. At the T5 level, a subcutaneous lesion in the interscapular region measures up to 9 × 8 mm with density of approximately +19 HU.
doc_impression:
1. A few solid pulmonary nodules. Given the presence of risk factors, follow-up CT of the chest may be performed in 12 months per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations. Pulmonary emphysema. Fibrotic bands with a few calcifications in the right upper lobe, possibly post-inflammatory (including post-tuberculous) in etiology; correlate with clinical history.

2. Mild coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

3. Aortic valve calcification.

4. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

5. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Transpedicular fixation at T1-T2. Decreased vertebral body density (CT signs of demineralization) at T11-L1.

6. Multiple calcified lesions in the breasts (partially included in the scan range) (oleogranulomas?); correlate with preferred imaging modalities. Subcutaneous lesion in the interscapular region at T5, possibly an atheroma; correlate with physical examination.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Bilateral pleuroapical scarring. In the apices of both lungs there are several areas of paraseptal emphysema. In the upper lobes and apices, there is moderate centrilobular emphysema. In both lungs there are scattered few cysts with lower lobe predominance. In the upper lobe of the right lung there are several ground glass areas with reticulations, bronchiectasis, strands to the pleura and emphysema at the periphery. The largest ground glass area measures 3.3 × 2.2 cm (image 70, series 5) with a few calcifications up to 7 mm. The ground glass area on image 46 measures 1.7 × 1.3 cm (image 70, series 5). Linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe, lingula, and in lung bases. There are gravitational changes bilaterally. In the right lower lobe there is a paravertebral area of osteophyte-induced fibrosis. A few solid pulmonary nodules are present in series 5 as follows: 
In the right middle lobe:
  - Subpleural solid nodule measures up to 5 mm (image 150).
In the right lower lobe:
  - Solid nodule measures up to 4 mm (image 137).
In the left upper lobe:
  - Calcified nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 90).
No pleural effusion. The trachea and main bronchi are patent. Mild bronchial wall thickening.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No masses or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. Mild mitral valve calcification. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left main, left anterior descending, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with moderate wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Transpedicular fixation at T1-T2. Decreased density of bone tissue in the vertebrae at T11-L1 to 110 HU (CT signs of demineralization). Degenerative changes in the left shoulder joint. In the breasts, multiple partially calcified lesions measure up to 9 mm, likely late fat necrosis. Correlate with the dedicated breast imaging.

Upper abdomen: In the lateral crus of the left adrenal gland there is a lesion of 1.1 cm along the short axis with a density -9 HU.
val_impression:
1. In the upper lobe of the right lung there are ground glass areas with reticulations and bronchiectasis, with a few calcifications, may correspond to fibrous changes (post-inflammatory). ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: Initial follow-up chest CT at 3-6 months to confirm persistence. If stable, follow-up chest CT every 2 years until 5 years. 
Two solid nodules of the right lung up to 5 mm. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional follow-up chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion. If unchanged, no further follow-up is necessary.

2. Mild paraseptal emphysema, moderate centrilobular emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has a history of lung cancer, known pulmonary nodules or lung mass, receives routine chest CTs/screening, or is receiving palliative care.

3. Scattered pulmonary cysts may be clinically insignificant or consistent with the signs of lymphangioleiomyomatosis.

4. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left main, left anterior descending, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

5. Mild mitral valve calcification. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

6. Left adrenal lesion with benign features (adenoma). ACR White Paper recommendations (Mayo-Smith, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1038-1044) suggest the following: Benign adenoma. No need for further imaging follow-up. Consider biochemical evaluation to determine functional status.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura:  
In the posterior parts of both lungs, mild reticular changes. In the anterior parts of the left lung and in lung bases of the right lung, discoid atelectasis.  
Along the interlobar pleura bilaterally, perifissural nodules, measuring up to 3 × 4 mm.  
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No masses or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Pericardial effusion, with maximum thickness up to 8 mm. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.  
Aortic valve calcification.  
Right coronary artery stenting.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the wall of the thoracic aorta. The main pulmonary artery is dilated up to 30 mm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 89 HU, T12 - 75 HU, L1 - up to 77 HU. Decreased vertebral body height: T4 up to 26%, T5 by 31%, T6 by 28%, T7 by 28%. Healing fracture of the right 9th rib and the left 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th, 6th, and 7th ribs.

Upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
doc_impression:
1. No nodules requiring follow-up per Fleischner Society recommendations.

2. Perifissural nodules in both lungs.

3. Pericardial effusion.

4. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Aortic valve calcification.

5. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.0 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

6. Decreased vertebral body density (CT signs of demineralization).

7. Decreased vertebral body height at T4, T5, T6, and T7.

8. Multiple healing rib fractures bilaterally.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: In the dorsal lungs gravitational changes, in the left lingula discoid atelectasis. Along the interlobar pleura bilaterally, perifissural nodules (perifissural lymph nodes) measure up to 4 mm, require no follow-up according to ACR Fleischner Society recommendations. A few scattered micronodules up to 2 mm in both lungs (series 5, slice 78, 95, 97, 102, 179). No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No masses or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: The heart is slightly enlarged due to increased size of the ventricles. Pericardial effusion, with maximum thickness up to 8 mm, increased slightly compared to Abdomen CT on July 1, 2025. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as severe. Aortic and mitral valve calcifications. Metallic foreign body in the right atrium, not identified on Abdomen CT on July 1, 2025.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. Multiple calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the wall of the thoracic aorta. The main pulmonary artery is dilated up to 30 mm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 89 HU, T12 - 75 HU, L1 - up to 77 HU. Multiple healing fractures of the right 9th rib and the left 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th, 6th, 7th, and 8th ribs.

Upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
val_impression:
1. A few scattered micronodules up to 2 mm in both lungs. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of lung cancer, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional follow-up chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion. If unchanged, no further follow-up is necessary.

2. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs.

3. Small-volume pericardial effusion, with minimal interval worsening compared with the prior study.

4. Aortic and mitral valve calcifications. Metallic foreign body in the right atrium, not identified on Abdomen CT on July 1, 2025.

5. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.0 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

6. Decreased vertebral body density (CT signs of demineralization/osteoporosis).

7. Multiple healing rib fractures bilaterally.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_protocol:
INDICATION: Pulmonary nodule.

COMPARISON: Abdomen CT on July 1, 2025.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 6 mGy; total DLP 244 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 1.

doc_findings:
Severe respiratory motion artifacts. Visualization is markedly limited.

Lungs and pleura: Considering the artifacts described above, reliable assessment of nodules per the 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations is not possible. No large nodules are identified. A few calcifications in the left lung measure up to 4 mm.

The contour of the right hemidiaphragm is deformed: In the anterior portions, a defect is noted measuring approximately 5.5 cm on the sagittal image, with herniation of abdominal organs (small bowel loops with mesentery, ascending and transverse colon, and omental fat) into the right hemithorax by nearly 1/2 of its volume, without evidence of ascitic fluid within the herniation sac.  
The right lung is compressed by the hernia predominantly in the lower segments in lung bases and in the right middle lobe. The right upper lobe shows hypoventilatory changes.

No pleural effusion.  
The trachea and main bronchi (except for the atelectatic areas described above) are patent.  
Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: The mediastinum is shifted to the left. No mass or lymphadenopathy is identified.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary calcification is identified.  
Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.  
Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with formation of small osteophytes. Decreased density of bone tissue in the vertebrae at the T11 level up to +131 HU.  
Healing fracture of the posterior segment of the right 10th rib.

Upper abdomen (except as described above): Unremarkable.
doc_impression:
1. Examination is limited by respiratory motion artifacts; detection of nodules and their assessment per Fleischner Society recommendations is not possible. CT findings of a large hernia of the right hemidiaphragm (Morgagni hernia) with herniation of abdominal organs into the right hemithorax.

2. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with small osteophytes. Decreased vertebral body density (CT signs of demineralization) at T11.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_findings:
Severe respiratory motion artifacts. Visualization is markedly limited.

Lungs and pleura: The membranous portion of the trachea prolapses into the lumen (study performed on incomplete inspiration). Severe respiratory artifacts. Considering the artifacts, reliable assessment of nodules per the 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations is not possible. No large nodule or consolidation is identified. In the right hemithorax, the diaphragm is difficult to visualize. There is herniation of abdominal organs (small bowel loops with mesentery, ascending and transverse colon, and omental fat) occupying nearly 1/2 of right hemithorax volume. The right lung is compressed by the hernia predominantly in the lower segments in lung bases and in the right middle lobe. No pleural effusion.  

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: The mediastinum is shifted to the left. No mass or lymphadenopathy is identified.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary calcification is identified.  

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.  

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with formation of small osteophytes. Decreased density of bone tissue in the vertebrae at the T11 level up to +131 HU. Healed fractures of the right 8th and 10th ribs.

Upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
val_impression:
1. CT findings of a large hernia of the right hemidiaphragm with herniation of abdominal organs into the right hemithorax. Compression atelectasis of the lower and middle lobes of the right lung, mediastinal shift to the left. No large pulmonary nodule or consolidation is identified.

2. No coronary calcification is identified.  

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
